section.title: Sons and Heirs
section.sectionRationale: Paul deepens the argument from heirs to sonship. Once we were like children under guardians, enslaved to the basic principles of the world, but when the time had fully come God sent His Son, born of a woman, to redeem us and give us adoption as sons, and He sent the Spirit of His Son crying Abba, Father. Paul then pleads with the Galatians not to turn back to slavery, appealing to their former love for him.
section.thesis: In the fullness of time God sent His Son to redeem those under the law and make them sons, and sent the Spirit of His Son into their hearts crying Abba, Father, so that to turn back to law and the weak principles of the world is to abandon sonship for slavery, which is why Paul pleads with the Galatians in the anguish of a parent.
section.frameNote: Three movements: from slaves to sons, redeemed by the Son sent in the fullness of time and given the Spirit crying Abba; the folly of turning back to weak and worthless principles and observing days; and Paul's personal, pleading appeal as a spiritual parent in the pains of childbirth.

units.label: From slaves to sons
units.note: God sent His Son and the Spirit of His Son

verses.text: What I am saying is that as long as the heir is a child, he is no different from a slave, although he is the owner of everything.
He is subject to guardians and trustees until the date set by his father.
So also, when we were children, we were enslaved under the basic {{stoicheia|principles}} of the world.
But when the time had fully come, God sent His Son, born of a woman, born under the law,
to redeem those under the law, that we might receive our {{huiothesia|adoption as sons}}.
And because you are sons, God sent the Spirit of His Son into our hearts, crying out, “{{abba|Abba}}, Father!”
So you are no longer a slave, but a son; and since you are a son, you are also an heir through God.

units.label: Do not turn back to slavery
units.note: weak and worthless principles, observing days

verses.text: Formerly, when you did not know God, you were slaves to those who by nature are not gods.
But now that you know God, or rather are known by God, how is it that you are turning back to those weak and worthless principles? Do you wish to be enslaved by them all over again?
You are observing special days and months and seasons and years!
I fear for you, that my efforts for you may have been in vain.

units.label: Paul's pleading appeal
units.note: the anguish of a spiritual parent

verses.text: I beg you, brothers, become like me, for I became like you. You have done me no wrong.
You know that it was because of an illness that I first preached the gospel to you.
And although my illness was a trial to you, you did not despise or reject me. Instead, you welcomed me as if I were an angel of God, as if I were Christ Jesus Himself.
What then has become of your blessing? For I can testify that, if it were possible, you would have torn out your eyes and given them to me.
Have I now become your enemy by telling you the truth?
Those people are zealous for you, but not in a good way. Instead, they want to isolate you from us, so that you may be zealous for them.
Nevertheless, it is good to be zealous if it serves a noble purpose, at any time, and not only when I am with you.
My children, for whom I am again in the pains of childbirth until Christ is {{morphoo|formed}} in you,
how I wish I could be with you now and change my tone, because I am perplexed about you.

stoicheia.gloss: The basic principles or elemental things, the ABCs of the world. The term can refer to elementary religious rules or to the spiritual powers behind them. Paul uses it for the whole realm of law and ritual religion that enslaves before Christ, and shockingly he groups a return to the law with the old pagan bondage: both are weak and worthless compared to sonship in Christ.

huiothesia.gloss: Adoption as sons, the legal act of placing someone as a full son with all the rights of an heir. God redeemed those under the law precisely so they might receive adoption. This is not merely forgiveness but the granting of family status: the redeemed are made sons and daughters of God, with full standing and a sure inheritance.

abba.gloss: Abba, the Aramaic word for father, the intimate address a child uses at home. The Spirit of the Son prompts believers to cry Abba, Father, the same word Jesus used in Gethsemane. It marks the tender, confident access of a child to a loving father, the opposite of the fear and distance of a slave under law.

morphoo.gloss: To form, to shape, to bring to full form, as an embryo takes shape in the womb. Paul longs for Christ to be formed in the Galatians, for the character and life of Christ to take full shape within them. He pictures himself again in the pains of childbirth until this happens, showing that the goal of the gospel is not mere decision but Christ fully formed in the believer.

basic.context: Paul pictures the difference between a child who is still like a slave and a grown son who inherits. He says that before Christ we were like minors, enslaved under religious rules. But at the right time God sent His Son to redeem us and make us sons, and He sent the Spirit so we can call God Abba, Father. To go back to law now is to trade sonship for slavery, and Paul pleads with them not to do it.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage combines rich doctrine, the sending of the Son and the Spirit, with raw pastoral emotion. Notice the movement from slaves to sons to heirs, and then the personal appeal. Read the doctrine and the pleading together: Paul argues because he loves them like a parent.
basic.originalLanguages: Before Christ we were enslaved under the stoicheia, the basic principles of the world. God sent His Son to give us huiothesia, adoption as sons, and sent the Spirit so we cry abba, Father, the intimate word for daddy. Paul longs for Christ to be morphoo, formed, fully shaped in them.
basic.typology: The fullness of time fulfills the long waiting of the Old Testament for the promised deliverer. God sending His Son, born of a woman, echoes the promise of the seed of the woman in Genesis. Adoption as sons fulfills Israel's calling as God's son, now extended to all who are in Christ.
basic.christ: God sent His Son, born of a woman, born under the law, to redeem those under the law. Christ enters our condition fully, keeps the law, and pays the price to make us sons. The Spirit He gives is the Spirit of His Son. Christ's sonship becomes the pattern and ground of ours, so that we share His cry, Abba, Father.
basic.god: God is the Father who sends both the Son and the Spirit, and who adopts slaves into His family as sons and heirs. He acts at just the right time, according to His plan. He is the one who knows us, verse 9, before we know Him. The whole passage reveals the heart of a Father who brings us near.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 8 speaks of the Spirit of adoption by whom we cry Abba, Father. John 1 says those who receive Christ are given the right to become children of God. Ephesians 1 says God predestined us for adoption through Christ.
basic.oneStory: The story moves from slavery to sonship. What Israel foreshadowed as God's son, and what humanity lost in Eden, is restored and surpassed in Christ. God sends His Son to redeem and His Spirit to assure, gathering a family who call Him Father, and to return to slavery would be to undo the gospel.

context.literary: The section opens with an illustration from inheritance law, then rises to the great statement of the sending of the Son and the Spirit in verses 4 to 6, one of the clearest Trinitarian texts in Paul. It shifts sharply in verse 8 to rebuke and then in verse 12 to a warm, wounded personal appeal, revealing Paul's pastoral heart.
context.historical: The Galatians were largely Gentile converts who had once served pagan gods. Now they were being drawn to the Jewish calendar of days, months, seasons, and years. Paul sees their move toward law observance as, astonishingly, a slide back toward the same kind of bondage they had left. The agitators were courting them to win their allegiance.
context.canonical: Verses 4 to 7 are a treasured summary of redemption and adoption, echoed in Romans 8 and Ephesians 1. The passage anchors the doctrines of the incarnation, redemption, adoption, and the gift of the Spirit, showing the united work of Father, Son, and Spirit in salvation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that God has made believers sons and heirs through the sending of His Son and Spirit, so that turning back to law and ritual is a return to slavery, and to plead pastorally with the Galatians out of love not to abandon the freedom of sonship.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 4 to 7 declare what God has done. The rebuke and appeal are prescriptive: do not turn back to slavery, and value the truth even when it is hard to hear. Paul's personal history with them is described to move them, not to set a rule.

debates.question: What are the basic principles of the world that enslave?

views.label: Elementary religious systems, law and ritual included
views.case: Paul groups the Jewish calendar with the old pagan bondage, treating any religion of rule keeping apart from Christ as an enslaving ABC compared to mature sonship.

views.label: Spiritual powers behind the systems
views.case: Some take the term for spiritual forces that stand behind false worship and legalism, from which Christ frees us.

debates.bearing: Either way, to turn from Christ to law or ritual as the ground of acceptance is to return to slavery; the freedom of sonship is found in Christ alone.

debates.question: Does observing special days condemn all Christian calendars?

views.label: It condemns law observance as a means of acceptance
views.case: Paul rebukes adopting the calendar as part of coming under the law to be justified, a return to bondage, not the mere marking of seasons in gratitude.

views.label: It forbids all religious calendars
views.case: Some read a total ban, but Paul elsewhere allows freedom in observing days to the Lord; the issue here is using observance to be justified.

debates.bearing: The danger is not marking time but trusting law observance for standing with God; sonship rests on Christ, not the calendar.

study.originalLanguages: Before Christ we lived under the stoicheia, the elementary principles of the world, a childhood of rules and bondage. The turning point is the sending of the Son to secure huiothesia, adoption as sons, full family status with the rights of an heir. The proof of that status is the Spirit of the Son in our hearts prompting the cry abba, Father, the intimate Aramaic word for a trusted father, the very word Jesus prayed. And Paul's pastoral longing is captured in morphoo, formed: he aches like a mother in labor until Christ is fully formed in them, for the gospel aims not at a moment's decision but at Christ taking shape in the believer.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the long Old Testament waiting for the promised deliverer, who comes at the appointed moment in God's plan.
Echoes the promise of the seed of the woman in Genesis 3:15, the deliverer come to redeem.
Fulfills Israel's calling as God's son, now extended to all in Christ who receive full family status and inheritance.

study.christ: God sent His Son, born of a woman, born under the law, entering our very condition to redeem those under the law. Christ keeps the law perfectly and pays the price of redemption so that slaves become sons. The Spirit given to believers is the Spirit of His Son, and the cry Abba, Father, is the echo of Christ's own prayer. His unique sonship becomes the ground and pattern of ours, and He is being formed in His people until they bear His likeness.
study.god: God is unveiled as Father who sends the Son and the Spirit, the whole Trinity at work in our salvation. He acts in the fullness of time, unhurried and precise in His plan. He does not merely pardon but adopts, granting slaves the standing of sons and heirs. And He knows us before we know Him, verse 9, so that our security rests on His knowing love. Here is a Father who draws near and brings the far off into His family.

crossReferences.note: The Spirit of adoption by whom we cry Abba, Father, making us children and heirs with Christ.
To all who received Him He gave the right to become children of God.
God predestined us for adoption as sons through Jesus Christ, according to His will.
The seed of the woman promised, echoed in the Son born of a woman to redeem.

study.oneStory: The Bible's story runs from slavery to sonship. Humanity lost its place in Eden, and Israel was called God's son yet remained under the law like a child. In the fullness of time God sent His Son to redeem and His Spirit to assure, gathering a family from every nation who cry Abba, Father. To slide back into law would be to leave the Father's house for the slave quarters, and Paul pleads with the Galatians as a parent for their freedom.

sermon.title: From Slavery to Sonship (Galatians 4:1 to 11)
sermon.why: Begg traces the move from slavery under the elementary principles to full sonship secured by the sending of the Son and the Spirit, and warns against turning back to bondage after being made sons and heirs of God.

quiz.q: What did God do when the time had fully come?

options.text: He sent His Son, born of a woman, to redeem us and make us sons.
options.note: Verses 4 and 5 state the sending of the Son and our adoption.

options.text: He gave a new and stricter law to keep.
options.note: He sent His Son to redeem those under the law, not a new law.

options.text: He left humanity to find its own way to Him.
options.note: God took the initiative by sending His Son.

options.text: He abandoned the promise to Abraham.
options.note: The sending of the Son fulfills the promise, not abandons it.

quiz.q: What shows that believers are truly sons of God?

options.text: God sent the Spirit of His Son into their hearts, crying Abba, Father.
options.note: Verse 6 makes the Spirit the seal of sonship.

options.text: Their careful observance of special days.
options.note: The Spirit, not calendar keeping, marks sonship.

options.text: Their descent from Abraham by birth.
options.note: Sonship comes through Christ and the Spirit, not physical descent.

options.text: Their perfect obedience to the law.
options.note: Sonship is God's gift, sealed by the Spirit, not earned by law.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the Galatians turning to law observance?

options.text: As turning back to weak and worthless principles and slavery.
options.note: Verse 9 calls it a return to bondage.

options.text: As a wise step forward in maturity.
options.note: Paul sees it as a step backward into slavery, not forward.

options.text: As a harmless matter of personal taste.
options.note: He fears his labor among them may be in vain, so it is not harmless.

options.text: As proof of their strong faith.
options.note: It endangers their faith rather than proving it.

quiz.q: What is Paul's aim for the Galatians in verse 19?

options.text: That Christ would be fully formed in them.
options.note: Paul labors like a parent until Christ is formed in them.

options.text: That they would simply feel more religious.
options.note: The goal is Christ formed in them, not mere religious feeling.

options.text: That they would become loyal to Paul personally.
options.note: His aim is Christ in them, not personal loyalty to himself.

options.text: That they would keep the calendar more strictly.
options.note: He wants Christ formed in them, not law observance.

quiz.q: What does adoption as sons give the believer?

options.text: Full family standing as a son and heir through God.
options.note: Verse 7 moves from son to heir through God.

options.text: A temporary status that must be renewed by works.
options.note: Adoption grants permanent family standing, not a status earned by works.

options.text: The right to look down on others.
options.note: Sonship humbles and unites; it is not a badge of superiority.

options.text: Nothing until the law is fully kept.
options.note: Sonship and inheritance are God's gift, received in Christ.

questions: The Spirit prompts you to cry Abba, Father. How does knowing God as an intimate Father, not a distant master, change the way you pray?
Paul says returning to law is a return to slavery. What rules or performance are you tempted to trust for your standing with God?
You are known by God before you ever knew Him. How does resting in God's knowing love steady you when your feelings waver?
Paul labors until Christ is formed in the Galatians. What would it look like for Christ to be more fully formed in your character this year?
Paul risked being seen as an enemy by telling the truth in love. Who in your life might need you to speak hard truth out of genuine love?

translationNotes.note: Basic principles renders stoicheia, the elementary things or elemental forces, as the BSB footnote notes; Paul applies it to enslaving religion, whether pagan ritual or law kept for justification.
Abba is the Aramaic word for father, retained in the Greek text; it conveys the intimate, trusting address of a child, the same word Jesus used in Gethsemane.
The BSB sets off a phrase with an em dash here; it is rendered with commas, with no change to the wording.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 4:1-20. The em dash in verse 18 has been rendered as commas to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged. Sermon verified this session from the Truth For Life series A Study in Galatians, Volume 1 by Alistair Begg; the movement spans 4:1-20 while the sermon covers 4:1-11, the sonship core of the passage.
